nativeRange.habitat: Wet pools, stagnant waters, and tidal river margins in the mid-Atlantic United States, with historical populations now most commonly reported in Florida

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate or aquasoil for densest growth
open midground or trimmed foreground space
moderate to strong lighting for compact form
stable water column with regular fertilisation

avoidWith: boisterous diggers that uproot stem plants

summary: Pearl Weed (Micranthemum micranthemoides) is a versatile bright-green stem plant native to the eastern United States. Generally regarded as easier to grow than Dwarf Baby Tears, it can be trimmed into a foreground carpet, shaped as a midground bush, or left to trail in the background.
careNotes:
Pearl Weed (Micranthemum micranthemoides) is a small-leaved stem plant native to the mid-Atlantic United States, with present-day populations most often reported in Florida. It is widely sold under the older trade name Hemianthus micranthemoides, and a closely related form circulates as Hemianthus glomeratus; modern taxonomy places all of these within Micranthemum, family Linderniaceae. The common name 'Baby Tears' is also applied to Hemianthus callitrichoides (Dwarf Baby Tears), but the two are distinct species — Pearl Weed produces larger stems of 5–15 cm with elongated leaves and is generally regarded as significantly easier to grow than Dwarf Baby Tears' millimetre-scale carpet. The 'pearl' in the name comes from the tiny oxygen bubbles the plant releases under bright light and CO2 injection, a process called pearling.

Pearl Weed is one of the more versatile aquascaping plants available. Under strong light with CO2, it can be trimmed into a tight foreground carpet; in the midground it forms dense bright-green bushes; and left untrimmed toward the back of the tank it grows as a trailing stem. It prefers temperatures of 19–28 °C, a pH of around 6.0–7.5, and soft to moderately hard water (gH 3–15 dGH). Moderate to strong lighting is recommended — under low light the plant tends to stretch vertically, producing longer gaps between leaves and bare lower stems; brighter conditions encourage compact, bushy growth. CO2 supplementation is not strictly required but generally improves growth density and helps prevent greyish-white lime deposits that can form on leaves in tanks without it. A nutrient-rich substrate together with regular liquid fertilisation that includes iron is recommended for best results. Trimming is essential for keeping this plant in shape: under good conditions growth can reach 5–8 inches per month, which typically calls for weekly cut-backs. The plant's dense, fine foliage provides useful cover for dwarf shrimp and small fish fry.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative and very simple: trim healthy stem tops just above a node and replant them ~1–2 cm apart in the substrate. Each cutting will root and branch into a new plant within days under good conditions. Mature creeping stems also self-root at nodes where they contact substrate, and mats can be lifted, divided, and replanted to expand coverage. No flowering / sexual propagation is needed for aquarium use.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica article on Hemianthus micranthemoides (also Hemianthus glomeratus, formerly Micranthemum micranthemoides). Provided: stem-plant classification, US/Florida origin, moderate-to-strong light requirement, CO2 recommendation (CO2-limited leaves form greyish-white lime deposits), fast growth, versatility across foreground/midground/background, 'advanced' care rating, and trim-and-replant propagation. Tropica's search page was the originally supplied URL; resolved to this article page for the canonical Tropica care content.
